description: Trigger discrimination for the internal write-path gateway (this skill) vs the external API/webhook contract (api-event-architect), the authorization policy it enforces (authorization-matrix-designer), the audit record schema it emits into (audit-log-architect), the internal event backbone its domain events ride (streaming-event-architect), and the standing sub-tenant scope axis (intra-tenant-scope-architect).

cases.prompt: Every protected mutation should go through one server-side path that authenticates the actor from the token, derives tenant scope from the database, dedups retries, executes, and emits an audit record — and clients must not be able to write around it.
cases.note: SEAM CASE (primary) — actor-from-token, server-derived scope, idempotency, transactional audit emission, and the no-bypass invariant are the command pipeline's mechanics. No external contract, no policy authoring, no record-schema design is requested.
cases.reason: The subject is the internal write pipeline and its no-bypass invariant. It ENFORCES policy and EMITS audit records but authors neither; it dispatches, it is not the external API.

cases.prompt: Design the public API surface: REST resource conventions, semantic versioning with a deprecation/sunset window, per-tenant and per-plan rate limits, and the signed webhook envelope partners subscribe to.
cases.note: SEAM CASE (primary, reverse) — versioning, rate limits, and partner webhooks are the promise made to outside integrators. The gateway is what those endpoints dispatch into, not the contract itself.
cases.reason: External resource/versioning/rate-limit/webhook contract is api-event-architect. command-gateway-architect owns the internal pipeline behind that surface.

cases.prompt: Define who can do what: platform vs tenant roles, object-level permissions for every tenant-owned resource, deny-by-default posture, and the negative tests proving forbidden actions fail.
cases.reason: Authoring the roles × permissions × resources model is authorization-matrix-designer. The gateway CALLS that model at its authorize step; it does not decide the rules.

cases.prompt: Design the audit trail: the event taxonomy, the record schema (actor, tenant, action, target, outcome, correlation id), append-only tamper-evidence, and retention.
cases.reason: The audit record schema, taxonomy, and integrity guarantees are audit-log-architect. The gateway EMITS records into that schema at its emit step; it does not design the record.

cases.prompt: The domain events our writes emit need an internal backbone: topics, partition keys, consumer groups, dead-letter handling, and replay after an outage.
cases.reason: Topics, partitions, DLQ, and replay for the internal event pipeline are streaming-event-architect. The gateway decides WHAT to emit and that it commits with the write; the transport it rides is a separate design.

cases.prompt: Inside a tenant, design the standing per-user scope grants and the row-filter predicate on every scoped table so users see only their site/region's rows while admins see all.
cases.note: SEAM CASE — the STANDING per-user scope axis and read-side row filter vs the gateway's execute-time server-derived write scope on a single mutation.
cases.reason: Designing the standing scope axis, grant model, and read-side row-filter predicate is intra-tenant-scope-architect. This skill derives a write's tenant/resource scope at execute-time inside the command pipeline; it does not design the standing scope model the reads honor.
